story:
Chandler claws up a rocky path, city shoes slipping, lungs rasping. Years of pints and whisky. A man who learned to talk to ghosts because the living wouldn’t have him. All that remains is the hunt.

Up ahead: Terrence Clark. Terry. A name said like a warning. A legend that made the city smaller and him larger. Chandler knows the lies that built that legend, yet still steps into its shadow. The myth is easier to face than the man.

The farmhouse sits half-kept, half-forgotten. A place where vanity is useless. Little here needs to impress. History breathes. Chandler can’t mourn it. He’s done with mourning.

Inside, Terry waits at a farmhouse table. Chair skewed, one leg out, all ownership and appetite. He has always eaten more than his share. But something is wrong. Gary—his shadow, his last loyal man—is dead. In the back seat of the Range Rover. Terry has chewed through rivals for decades, clipped cubs before they grew, called surprise a nuisance and moved on. Not today. There’s a metallic taste. His own blood. He grins at the thought of a worthy successor; only one man ever came close.

The Range Rover is show-muscle in mud. The farmhouse door stands ajar. Too easy. A bad invitation. Chandler’s hackles rise. He enters anyway. No floorboard creak. Just the thick hush that always gathers around Terry.

They circle with words. “What, born in a barn?” Terry says. “What kept you, Ray?” Terry adds, slicing with that nickname. Raymond Chandler. Fiction. A taunt that stuck because Terry wanted it to. They trade jabs about morgues and football, about bodies and proof. Then the tell: blood on Terry’s teeth, slow breaths, the weight in his eyelids. Not just age. Damage.

“You need an ambulance?” Chandler asks. Terry waves it off. Pride won’t kneel. Neither of them can let the past loosen its grip. Two old fighters leaning on each other because the bell keeps them upright. The ring defines them; outside the ropes is nothing but ghosts.

Silence thickens. It’s the brother of the silence at the door. Chandler knows both. For two decades he drew borders. Terry stayed mostly inside them. A lesser evil contained by a stubborn cop who never quite quit. Chandler told himself the city needed that truce. He also knows Terry is his animal—and he is Terry’s. Teeth dulled, but still bared.

Terry tilts his head. “You don’t like change, do you?” He sees too much. He always has. “We’re twins,” he says, amused and hoarse. “You could never kill me. It’d be like killing yourself.” Chandler plays the philosopher because it’s easier than punching a man already on the ropes. Too late, he hears the trap in Terry’s last question: why did he endure?

The answer enters the room.

DCI Gill Trotter takes Chandler’s arm, moves him back. Terry’s eyes meet hers. A flicker. A ghost of a smile. His farewell is betrayal. His choice, not Chandler’s.

“Take the shot.”

No pause. Terry straightens, then drops. The old lion, finished.

Chandler never saw it coming. He watched the cubs—young pretenders—rise and fall. He didn’t watch the lioness. Patient. Playing both games. She stepped up to the king and took everything in one clean act. And this is only the beginning. A new story opens, and Chandler isn’t in it.
